Why Continuous Monitoring Is Essential for Modern Supply Chain Compliance
=========================================================================

December 22, 2024

Continuous monitoring refers to the systematic process of continuously assessing and analyzing the performance and compliance of a supply chain in real time. This proactive approach is crucial in today's fast-paced markets, where delays or deviations can lead to significant disruptions. By implementing [continuous supply chain monitoring](https://www.certa.ai/about), companies gain instant insights into their operations, enabling them to anticipate and address potential issues before they escalate.

The Role of Continuous Monitoring in Ensuring Supply Chain Compliance
---------------------------------------------------------------------

### Identifying Compliance Gaps in Global Operations

In global operations, regulatory frameworks vary significantly across borders. Continuous tracking helps companies stay abreast of these changes by providing real-time insights into compliance status across all operational territories. This proactive approach is essential not just for adhering to legal standards but also for maintaining the integrity and reputation of the business. Implementing such monitoring systems ensures compliance issues are identified and rectified before they can escalate into more severe problems.

![Image 11: Continuous supply chain monitoring presentation with team analyzing bar and pie charts on a large screen](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62a9b4f36a23c435c9b9f3cc/678e44c1414167f5d0623b6c_678e44b75d3e719bd3176b7d_continuous%2520supply%2520chain%2520monitoring.webp)

### Real-Time Monitoring for Regulatory Adherence

The dynamic nature of global markets necessitates [real-time monitoring of supply chains](https://www.certa.ai/module/supplier-onboarding). Monitoring form is crucial for ensuring that all parts of the supply chain adhere to regulatory requirements that may change rapidly. Real-time data collection and analysis allow businesses to adjust their operations to maintain compliance as market conditions or regulations evolve. This capability is especially crucial in pharmaceuticals and food and beverage industries, where compliance with safety standards is directly tied to public health.

### Enhancing Transparency Across Supply Chain Networks

Transparency is fundamental in modern supply chains, and monitoring supply chain risks plays a critical role in achieving it. By continuously tracking goods, transactions, and interactions across the supply chain, companies can give stakeholders visibility into sourcing, labor practices, and environmental impacts. Such a level of transparency helps meet regulatory compliance and boosts trust among consumers and partners, which is increasingly becoming a competitive differentiator in business.

### Proactive Risk Detection and Mitigation Strategies

Continuous compliance in supply chains involves proactive risk detection and mitigation to manage potential disruptions effectively. Leveraging data analytics and monitoring technology can predict and mitigate risks ranging from supplier failures to logistical delays. A proactive approach also helps maintain smooth operations and avoid costs associated with supply chain disruptions. It also ensures that compliance standards are consistently met, safeguarding the company against legal penalties and loss of goodwill.

### Reducing Manual Errors with Automated Monitoring

Automated systems can continuously check for discrepancies in invoices, shipments, and regulatory filings much more efficiently than human counterparts. This not only speeds up the compliance verification process but also frees up human resources to focus on more strategic aspects of supply chain management. Automation in monitoring ensures higher accuracy in compliance-related tasks, enhancing overall operational efficiency.

Benefits of Supply Chain Monitoring
-----------------------------------

### Improved Supply Chain Risk Management

Continuous monitoring facilitates robust [supply chain risk management strategies](https://www.certa.ai/resources/supply-chain-risk-management). By monitoring operations around the clock, businesses can detect anomalies that may signal risks such as supply shortages, delivery delays, or quality control issues. This vigilance allows for immediate action, potentially averting significant disruptions affecting service levels and customer satisfaction. Effective risk management through continuous monitoring safeguards operational continuity and supports long-term business resilience.

### Strengthened Supplier Accountability

[Monitoring supply chain performance](https://www.certa.ai/) keeps suppliers accountable for their commitments and compliance requirements. Oversight ensures suppliers adhere to contractual terms regarding quality, delivery timelines, and ethical standards. Enhanced accountability fosters a more reliable and compliant supply chain network, critical for maintaining product quality and brand reputation. It provides leverage in negotiations and supplier evaluations, promoting better supplier relationships and performance.

### Enhanced Decision-Making with Real-Time Data

Immediate insight into supply chain dynamics allows management to respond swiftly to market changes or operational challenges. Real-time data analysis supports strategic planning and reactive measures, ensuring that decisions are based on the most current and comprehensive information available, optimizing outcomes and competitive advantage.

Tools and Technologies Enabling Continuous Supply Chain Monitoring
------------------------------------------------------------------

### Overview of Supply Chain Monitoring Software

These platforms provide a comprehensive view of all activities, from procurement to delivery, ensuring that every step meets compliance standards. These tools offer actionable insights by integrating data from various sources, helping companies optimize their operations. Additionally, monitoring software often features trend analysis and forecasting capabilities, further enhancing operational strategies and compliance management.

### Supply Chain Visibility Tools for Compliance

Visibility is key to ensuring compliance with regulations and standards, particularly in industries where safety and quality are paramount. Moreover, these tools facilitate communication across the supply chain, enabling quick responses to operational challenges and maintaining uninterrupted compliance.

### Role of Machine Learning and Analytics

Both play a transformative role in modern supply chain technologies. These digital solutions can predict trends and identify patterns that human analysts might overlook. Machine learning algorithms can anticipate supply chain disruptions and suggest preventive measures by processing vast amounts of data. This predictive capability is invaluable for maintaining continuous compliance and operational efficiency in dynamic market conditions.

### IoT and Cloud Platforms

Automating supply chain monitoring is significantly enhanced by IoT (Internet of Things) and cloud technologies. IoT devices collect data directly from the physical elements of the supply chain, such as containers and vehicles, providing real-time updates on their status and conditions. Cloud platforms then analyze this data, offering accessible insights from anywhere worldwide. Such integration of IoT and cloud technology not only increases data accuracy but also speeds up compliance monitoring.

### Setting Up Dashboards and Real-Time Alerts

Setting up dashboards and real-time alerts is critical in optimizing real-time compliance monitoring. These systems serve as the operational nerve center, providing a user-friendly interface that displays key performance indicators (KPIs) and real-time data. Here is a breakdown of the process:

1.   **Selection of Key Metrics:** Choosing the right key performance indicators (KPIs) is the foundation of an effective monitoring system. These metrics should align with an organization’s strategic goals and operational priorities. Common KPIs include supplier delivery accuracy, order fulfillment rates, production downtime, and inventory turnover. For compliance-driven industries, additional metrics such as regulatory adherence rates, audit pass rates, and incident response times may be necessary. The selection process should involve cross-departmental input to ensure that the dashboard reflects the needs of all stakeholders. Businesses can eliminate unnecessary data clutter and prioritize meaningful insights by focusing on the most critical KPIs. A targeted approach improves decision-making, helping companies proactively address risks and streamline supply chain performance.
2.   **Integration of Data Sources:** Without proper integration, organizations risk data silos that can lead to inefficiencies and blind spots in supply chain management. Modern cloud-based platforms enable seamless data consolidation from enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems, Internet of Things (IoT) devices, and third-party vendor portals. Automated data syncing ensures that information remains up to date, reducing reliance on manual reporting. Businesses gain a comprehensive view of their supply chain monitoring tools by consolidating data from various sources into a single platform. This allows for better coordination and improved response times to potential disruptions.
3.   **Customization of Dashboard Layouts:** To maximize usability, dashboards should be customized based on user roles and specific operational needs. Executives may require high-level summaries with trend analysis, while logistics managers may need real-time tracking details and inventory fluctuations. Customization also involves setting up graphical representations such as heatmaps, trend charts, and performance comparisons to enhance data visualization. Many platforms allow businesses to define metric thresholds, enabling quick identification of underperforming areas. A well-structured layout improves navigation, ensuring critical information is immediately visible and accessible. 
4.   **Implementation of Real-Time Alerts:** Setting up real-time alerts ensures that critical changes in supply chain performance trigger immediate action. These alerts can be configured based on predefined thresholds, such as a sudden drop in supplier fulfillment rates, a regulatory compliance violation, or an unexpected inventory shortage. Alerts can be delivered via multiple channels, including SMS, email, and push notifications, ensuring that the right personnel receive timely updates. Automated alerts reduce reliance on manual oversight, allowing businesses to respond proactively to potential risks. Also, alerts can be categorized based on priority levels, helping teams focus on the most urgent issues first. By integrating automated alert systems, organizations improve operational responsiveness, minimize downtime, and enhance risk mitigation efforts.
5.   **Ongoing Evaluation and Updates:** Regularly reviewing dashboard settings and alert configurations ensures the system remains aligned with evolving supply chain strategies. This process should include periodic audits to assess dashboard effectiveness, feedback collection from end users, and updates to reflect regulatory changes. Continuous refinement enables businesses to incorporate new technologies, such as AI-driven analytics, to enhance predictive insights. Regular updates also help prevent alert fatigue by fine-tuning notification criteria, ensuring that personnel receive meaningful and actionable alerts. Organizations can stay ahead of emerging risks and optimize supply chain performance over time by maintaining a dynamic and adaptable monitoring system.

Implementing dashboards and real-time alerts is a strategic investment that enhances supply chain visibility and risk management. These tools empower organizations to respond quickly to disruptions, maintain compliance, and drive efficiency, ultimately contributing to a more resilient and agile supply chain.

Strategies for Implementing Continuous Monitoring in Supply Chains
------------------------------------------------------------------

### Integrating Monitoring Systems

This strategy involves syncing data from diverse sources such as procurement, logistics, and sales to create a unified view of the supply chain. This integration allows for seamless data flow and improved communication between departments, ensuring that everyone accesses the same accurate and timely information, which is critical for maintaining continuous compliance in supply chains.

### Leveraging Digital Tools for Seamless Data Collection

Utilizing digital tools for supply chain compliance facilitates the seamless collection and analysis of data across the supply chain. These tools automate the capture of transactional data, supplier performance records, and compliance reports, minimizing the risk of errors inherent in manual processes. The digital collection ensures that data is current and historically accurate, essential for trend analysis and making informed decisions.

![Image 12: Real-time monitoring of supply chains displayed with financial graphs and charts on a tablet and laptop](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62a9b4f36a23c435c9b9f3cc/678e44c1414167f5d0623b66_678e44a7f23c5432ea5a7d2e_real-time%2520monitoring%2520of%2520supply%2520chains.webp)

### Training Teams on Supply Chain Monitoring Best Practices

Practical training ensures that all employees understand the importance of compliance and are equipped to utilize the monitoring systems effectively. Here is how businesses can approach this training:

*   **Develop Comprehensive Training Modules:** A well-structured training program begins with the creation of detailed modules that cover all aspects of supply chain monitoring. These modules should include step-by-step instructions on data entry, analysis, and reporting to ensure that employees fully understand the functionality of the monitoring system. Incorporating case studies and real-world examples helps trainees see how monitoring impacts operational efficiency and compliance. Training modules should address troubleshooting common issues, equipping employees with problem-solving skills. 
*   **Customize Training for Different Roles:** Not all employees will interact with monitoring tools in the same way, making role-based training essential. For example, supply chain managers may need in-depth data analytics and compliance tracking knowledge, while warehouse staff may focus on real-time reporting and system inputs. Customizing training based on job responsibilities ensures that employees receive relevant and practical instruction tailored to their daily tasks. Role-specific training also enhances engagement, as employees are more likely to retain information when they see its direct relevance to their work. Organizations can develop department-specific workshops or provide on-the-job training to reinforce learning. 
*   **Incorporate Hands-On Learning:** Practical, hands-on experience is crucial in ensuring employees fully grasp monitoring processes. Instead of relying solely on theoretical instruction, organizations should integrate real-life simulations that allow employees to practice using monitoring tools. These exercises can include mock compliance audits, simulated data entry tasks, and interactive problem-solving scenarios. Training sessions should also incorporate team-based exercises where employees work together to resolve monitoring-related challenges. Hands-on learning fosters confidence, enabling employees to navigate the system efficiently in real-world situations. Shadowing programs, where less experienced employees learn directly from seasoned professionals, can reinforce learning and encourage collaboration. 
*   **Continuous Learning Opportunities:** Real-time monitoring for the supply chain landscape is constantly evolving, with new technologies, regulations, and best practices emerging regularly. To keep teams well-informed, organizations should implement ongoing training programs that provide updates on the latest developments. Regular refresher courses ensure that employees remain proficient in using monitoring tools and understand any system upgrades. Webinars, industry workshops, and e-learning platforms can be leveraged to facilitate continuous learning. Additionally, organizations should establish a knowledge-sharing culture where employees exchange insights and experiences to improve collective expertise. Continuous education strengthens monitoring capabilities and demonstrates a commitment to professional development, increasing employee engagement and retention.
*   **Feedback and Assessment:** Organizations should implement feedback mechanisms like surveys, quizzes, and performance assessments to measure training impact. Conducting periodic evaluations allows trainers to identify gaps in understanding and adjust content accordingly. Encouraging employees to share their insights and challenges helps refine training approaches to better meet their needs. By consistently gathering and analyzing feedback, businesses can enhance their training programs, ensuring that employees remain proficient and confident in their monitoring responsibilities.

Investing in employee training is fundamental to the importance of supply chain monitoring systems. Well-trained teams enhance operational efficiency, minimize risks, and ensure regulatory compliance in supply chains, ultimately contributing to a more resilient and well-managed supply chain.

![Image 13: Supply chain compliance discussion during a meeting with laptops and a presentation on company threats](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62a9b4f36a23c435c9b9f3cc/678e44c1414167f5d0623b69_678e4493cf612e66d10fadfc_supply%2520chain%2520compliance.webp)

### Collaborating with Stakeholders

Collaboration with stakeholders is essential for ensuring compliance in global supply chains. This involves regular communication with suppliers, partners, and regulatory bodies to ensure all parties are aligned with compliance standards and operational expectations. Effective collaboration helps identify potential compliance issues before they become problematic, fostering a proactive approach to supply chain management.

The adoption of continuous monitoring is not merely a regulatory necessity but a strategic advantage that can dictate the success and resilience of modern supply chain compliance monitoring. Companies that embrace this approach will be better equipped to navigate the challenges of a rapidly changing global marketplace, ensuring sustainability and profitability in the long term. The future will belong to those who can turn the data generated by continuous monitoring into actionable insights and strategic foresight, transforming challenges into opportunities for growth and innovation.

# Continuously monitor your supply chain for enhanced resilience

[RobobAI](https://blog.robobai.com/author/robobai) :

Jul 16, 2024

![Continuously monitor your supply chain for enhanced resilience](https://blog.robobai.com/hubfs/test-campaigns/risk-monitoring/continuously-monitor-your-supply-chain-for-enhanced-resilience-blog.jpg)

The ability to continuously monitor supply chain risks is essential for maintaining resilience and continuity. By using advanced monitoring software platforms and proactive strategies, businesses can bolster their readiness to navigate complex challenges and sustain operational stability. In this post, we explore the pivotal role of [continuous monitoring](https://www.securityforum.org/in-the-news/the-challenge-of-continuous-assurance-for-supply-chains/) in enhancing supply chain resilience and empowering organizations to promptly detect and address emerging risks.

## Benefits of continuous monitoring

Continuous monitoring provides numerous advantages that help businesses maintain a robust and resilient supply chain. Here are some key benefits:

### Improved supply chain visibility and transparency

Continuous monitoring tools offer insights into every aspect of the supply chain, from raw material procurement to product delivery. This improved visibility enables businesses to track and analyze supply chain activities, ensuring that every link in the chain operates efficiently. Transparency is essential for identifying inefficiencies, optimizing processes, and maintaining a clear view of supplier performance.

For example, continuous monitoring can reveal bottlenecks in the production process or delays in shipping, allowing companies to address these issues before they escalate. According to [a 2023 study by Deloitte](https://www2.deloitte.com/xe/en/insights/focus/supply-chain/issues-in-global-supply-chain.html), companies with high supply chain visibility reported significantly higher revenue growth compared to those with low visibility.

### Early detection of disruptions

One of the most significant advantages of continuous monitoring is the early detection of disruptions that could impact operations. By continuously analyzing data from various sources, monitoring systems can identify patterns and anomalies that may indicate potential risks. This early warning system enables businesses to take proactive measures to mitigate the impact of disruptions.

For instance, if a supplier is experiencing negative press for a breach of ethical standards, continuous monitoring can alert the company to seek alternative suppliers or renegotiate terms before a supply chain break occurs. Early detection allows for swift action, reducing downtime and minimizing financial losses.

### Enhanced agility in responding to emerging threats

Continuous monitoring equips businesses with the agility to [respond quickly to emerging threats](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/377340721_Risk_Evaluation_and_Management_Involved_in_Supply_Chain_Management). In a rapidly changing business environment, the ability to adapt and respond to new risks is crucial for maintaining operational stability. Continuous monitoring provides the data and insights needed to make informed decisions and implement effective response strategies.

## How to choose the right tool to monitor your supply chain risk

Selecting the right [continuous monitoring tool](https://robobai.com/third-party-risk-management) is crucial for maximizing the ability to quickly identify potential third-party risks across your supply chain. Here are some key features and capabilities to look for:

### Automation capabilities for seamless data management and analysis

Automation and continuous monitoring are critical features in supply chain risk management tools. [Automated data collection and analysis](https://www.forbes.com/sites/forbestechcouncil/2024/05/07/smart-supply-chain-management-implementing-iot-technologies-to-optimize-supply-chain-visibility/) streamline the monitoring process, reducing the need for manual intervention and minimizing the risk of errors. Automation capabilities allow businesses to process large volumes of data quickly and efficiently to identify and mitigate risk across their supply chain.

For example, an automated system can continuously monitor supplier activities, such as media reports of sanctions or modern slavery breaches. This enables organizations to proactively manage potential financial and reputational damage.

### Proactive alert systems to prioritize critical issues

Proactive alert systems are essential for prioritizing critical issues and ensuring that they receive immediate attention. These systems can be configured to send notifications based on predefined criteria, such as expiry dates of mandatory supplier compliance documentation.

For instance, if a key supplier has breached regulatory or compliance requirements, the monitoring system can send a notification, prompting the business to assess the potential impact and initiate contingency plans. Proactive alerts enable organizations to stay ahead of potential disruptions and take timely actions to mitigate risks.

### Scalability to accommodate growing supply chain complexities

As businesses grow and supply chains become more complex, the monitoring tool must be scalable to accommodate increased data volumes and additional monitoring requirements. Scalability ensures that the monitoring system can handle the evolving needs of the business without compromising performance or reliability.

A scalable risk management platform should access various third-party data sources, including sanctions, modern slavery and ESG, providing a comprehensive view of supply chain risk. This flexibility allows businesses to expand their monitoring capabilities as needed, ensuring continuous visibility and control.

### Risk alerts and notifications for greater responsiveness

Risk alerts and notifications are critical for maintaining awareness of potential risks to the supply chain. These features enable businesses to monitor for specific risk factors, such as geopolitical instability, regulatory changes, or cybersecurity threats, and receive timely notifications when risks are detected.

Simon Ellis, Chief Sales Officer at RobobAI, says these capabilities provide real value to organizations looking to protect supply chain integrity.

“RobobAI’s unique supplier risk management solution includes continuous monitoring and risk alert capabilities that provide notifications of potential threats to the supply chain.  This enables large organizations to identify emerging risks quickly, and take proactive measures to safeguard their operations,” he said.

## Constant vigilance: monitored supply chain gains

[Continuous supplier monitoring](https://robobai.com/supplychain-risk) is necessary for enhancing resilience and maintaining operational stability. The benefits of continuous monitoring include improved visibility and transparency, early detection of compliance breaches, and enhanced agility in responding to emerging threats. By selecting the right monitoring tool with automation capabilities, proactive alert systems, scalability, and robust risk alerts, businesses can ensure they are well-equipped to navigate the complexities of the modern supply chain.

Implementing continuous monitoring is not just a technological upgrade; it is a strategic move that empowers businesses to stay ahead of potential risks, optimize their supply chain operations, and maintain a competitive edge in the marketplace. As supply chains continue to evolve, the importance of continuous monitoring will only grow, making it an indispensable component of effective supply chain management.

Managing supplier compliance is resource-intensive, ineffective and costly - and in today’s complex environment, it can be easy to engage with risky or sanctioned entities that pose serious legal and reputational risks. Supply chain risk management (SCRM) involves assessing and problem-solving for all types of risks surrounding supply sources and their [potential risk areas](https://www.auditboard.com/blog/assessing-weak-links-supply-chain/) — including suppliers, third-party dependencies, physical locations, and more. Handled properly, supply chain risk management integrates with a company’s overall [risk management strategy](/blog/10-risk-management-strategies) and provides another layer of oversight, ideally reducing the likelihood and/or severity of risk exposures.

Today, effective SCRM has a major impact on an organization’s risk and security posture as the supply chain becomes increasingly digitized and supply chain attacks [become more common](https://www.fortinet.com/resources/cyberglossary/supply-chain-attacks). At least [89% of companies](https://www.gartner.com/en/supply-chain/insights/supply-chain-risk-management) have experienced some kind of supplier risk event in the last five years. On the flip side, building supply chain resilience can give organizations a competitive advantage in the market, enabling them to prevent the realization of supplier risks and respond to events quickly and decisively. Building supply chain resilience is indeed a key strategy for organizations seeking to mitigate these risks. In this article, we will outline today’s top risks to the supply chain and review how to strengthen your company’s supply chain risk management response plans.

## What Is Supply Chain Risk Management?

SCRM is the process by which an enterprise takes strategic steps to identify, assess, and mitigate all risks in its business’s end-to-end supply chain.

Since a supply chain encompasses all the materials, resources, and processes involved in producing and delivering a product, any disruption within it can significantly impact a company’s financial performance. These disruptions can slow down production, delay distribution, or increase costs, ultimately affecting the organization’s bottom line. An SCRM plan implements processes after evaluating both every day and edge case risks along the supply chain, with the ultimate goal of reducing company vulnerabilities and ensuring the continuity of business.

With cyberattacks on supply chains becoming more frequent and often happening in real-time, securing these networks has never been more critical. The growing threat has elevated the focus on cyber supply chain risk management, making it a top priority for businesses aiming to protect their operations and assets.NIST, the U.S.’s National Institute of Standards and Technology, [released updated guidance in May 2022](https://www.nist.gov/news-events/news/2022/05/nist-updates-cybersecurity-guidance-supply-chain-risk-management) focusing on building cyber supply chain resilience ([NIST Special Publication 800-161 Revision 1](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-161r1.pdf)). This, plus organizations’ growing reliance on suppliers and third-party providers for information and communications technology (ICT), including critical infrastructure, means that a successful attack on a company’s cyber supply chain can be devastating, for revenue and reputation. Thus, cyber supply chain risk management forms a key piece of the overall SCRM picture, and should be incorporated into an organization’s supplier [risk management strategy](https://www.auditboard.com/blog/10-risk-management-strategies/). Incorporating cyber supply chain risk into an SCRM program involves taking into account the suppliers and providers in the cyber supply chain, whether that’s a Cloud Service Provider or a SaaS vendor, and addressing risks associated with those third parties.

## Why Is Supply Chain Risk Management Important?

Proper SCRM is good for business. After all, [what is risk management](/blog/10-risk-management-strategies) in supply chain optimization about, if not finding efficiencies, cutting costs, and mitigating vulnerabilities? Done well, supply chain management works to implement the most streamlined and least expensive supply chain process for your business within your risk tolerance levels. Doing a thorough risk assessment is mission critical, as the just-in-time nature of today’s business flows means if any link in the supply chain breaks, the costs can be high in terms of both time and money.

Remember, the risk management discipline is all about anticipating “What could go wrong?” and applying the right level of risk mitigation to those potential scenarios. Prevention is the best medicine, and SCRM incorporates that philosophy, aiming to stop supply chain disruptions before they can happen. In the event that supply chains do get disrupted, organizations equipped with strong SCRM programs will ideally already have mitigation strategies in place to expedite decision-making.

With the increasing globalization of supply chains, the stakes are higher than ever. National security concerns—especially with organizations like the Department of Homeland Security playing a key role in the [U.S. supply chain](https://www.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/2023-03/Supply%20Chain%20Security%20Final%20Report%2003162023.pdf)—combine with regulatory and sustainability demands, turning SCRM  from a mere advantage to an absolute necessity. Businesses now face complex risks that span borders, regulations, and environmental impact, making robust SCRM practices essential for resilience and long-term success.

## Supply Chain Risks and Challenges

The extreme Interdependency of today’s global marketplace means [supply chain troubles](https://www.auditboard.com/blog/challenges-facing-risk-management/) can quickly snowball from one industry into the next. SCRM  issues include weather, resource shortages, and technology dependencies — like the chip shortage that has impacted industries ranging from video games to car sales. Shipping container chaos and cybersecurity threats are also on the list of SCRM  risk factors to consider. The development of new and emerging technologies, like generative artificial intelligence (AI), poses another challenge and source of risk for organizations looking to leverage new information systems and technology in their supply chain.

**Image: Supply Chain Risks and Challenges**

### 1. Extreme Weather

The physical logistics of the supply chain mean storms, icy roads, mudslides, and other extreme weather events can cause significant problems. Unexpected snows in Texas caused [food supply chain gaps](https://www.supplychaindive.com/news/winter-storm-texas-food-grocery-heb-supply-chains-logistics/595354/) that impacted warehousing, storage, and food delivery to retailers. Another dramatic example is the unforgettable image of the Ever Given container ship stuck sideways in the Suez Canal in March 2021. High winds were blamed for knocking the ship askew and causing the jam. Despite tugboats and diggers working tirelessly to free the ship, it wasn’t pulled free until the seventh day — after a full moon caused a high tide. Once it was out, nearly [400 ships carrying a billion dollars](https://www.nytimes.com/2021/03/29/world/middleeast/suez-canal-ever-given.html) worth of international commerce could finally continue their journey.

The effect of extreme weather on supply chains is amplified for companies that have global supply chains, with localized weather events potentially disrupting deliveries of raw materials and goods.

### 2. Technology Dependencies

A microchip shortage started at the beginning of 2020, as consumers created a [surge in demand for home electronics](https://www.cnbc.com/2021/02/10/whats-causing-the-chip-shortage-affecting-ps5-cars-and-more.html) — likely due to requirements for remote school and the need to have work-from-home capabilities. The ripple effect caused GM, Ford, Honda, Chrysler, and others to blame 2021’s slowed vehicle production on the chip shortage. Similarly, Sony said the lack of chip availability was the reason why it’s still hard to buy a PlayStation 5 more than a year after the console’s November 2020 launch. It wasn’t until [2023](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-06-30/car-prices-are-starting-to-ease-as-pandemic-supply-chain-issues-fade) that car manufacturers and electronics companies began to recover from the chip shortage and ramp up production once again.

Microchip shortages are just one example of how supply chains can be disrupted by technology dependencies. Other examples include dependencies on Cloud Service Providers or SaaS providers. [An outage on the supplier’s side](https://www.cnn.com/2023/06/13/tech/amazon-web-services-outage/index.html) can take out an entire business by shutting down their websites, e-commerce sites, and even potentially their intranet. Outages and technological disruptions often lead to outcry on social media outlets, affecting the reputational standing of a company.

### 3. Shipping Container Upheavals

With supply chains disrupted and trade routes altered, the flow of shipping containers was drastically changed. Shipping companies found it was more profitable to send empty containers back across the ocean for refilling instead of returning full containers that had been refilled in the United States before being sent back overseas. This disruption to the usual back-and-forth impacted trade and caused massive delivery issues and price increases. A 2022 study reported that due to changes in the typical container cycle — plus the added issues of congested ports and delayed unloading — a typical container will now spend [20% longer in transit](https://theconversation.com/supply-chains-in-2022-shortages-will-continue-but-for-some-sellers-the-problem-will-be-too-much-stock-174085) than before the pandemic. In addition, shipping prices on major East-West trade routes [jumped by 80%](https://www.drewry.co.uk/supply-chain-advisors/supply-chain-expertise/world-container-index-assessed-by-drewry) year-over-year — and that is still holding true as of September 2024.

Geopolitical issues have contributed to major disruptions in the global supply chain, from the microchip shortage mentioned earlier to exports of grain from Ukraine.

### 4. Cybersecurity Threats

Data leaks, operational disruptions, and malware attacks are the most common cyber risks associated with supply chain issues. Attacks can come from anywhere, and it’s mission-critical that every organization stays current on its [information security protocols](https://www.auditboard.com/blog/importance-of-information-security-in-organization/).

With many organizations now leveraging powerful cloud services or SaaS solutions, cybersecurity attacks via third parties and suppliers are an attractive option for hackers and threat actors. Sometimes, these third parties are in the private sector and not subject to the same regulatory and security requirements as the purchaser, or they may simply be smaller companies that do not have the personnel for a fully-fledged information security program. These matters should be accounted for during due diligence and prior to procurement.

## What Are Best Practices for Managing Supply Chain Risk?

The best way to prepare for the many risks and challenges to the supply chain is to have a solid SCRM framework. Using multiple suppliers, finding closer sources, knowing your risk tolerance, and modeling dire scenarios are all tactics and methodologies for building a solid risk management plan.

### 1. Source Multiple Suppliers

The fallout of the pandemic exposed significant gaps in global retail and manufacturing supply chains. When output delays first started, some retailers shifted to multi-source modeling, which creates a system of backup suppliers if a single supplier loses access to a product. Keeping  an SCRM plan top of mind, an important consideration when sourcing suppliers is to look for one that produces out of multiple locations — that way, they aren’t subject to a single point of failure in the case of an environmental event. Both small businesses and large enterprises can significantly benefit from sourcing from multiple suppliers. I’ve seen critical operations, such as new employee onboarding or system implementations, delayed because a single supplier failed to deliver the necessary equipment or provided insufficient quantities. Diversifying suppliers helps avoid these disruptions and ensures smoother operations.

Maintaining a good relationship with suppliers and establishing a baseline of supply chain metrics can help organizations forecast future needs and ensure that they have the right suppliers in place to meet demand.

### 2. Establish for Nearshore Sources

How does an organization manage risks when those risks are out of their control, like force majeures or natural disasters? Unfortunately, none of us have superhuman control over the weather — which is a major factor in supply chain logistics.

Instead, companies can find solutions by choosing suppliers and distributors located closer to their main operations, gaining advantages from reduced distance and quicker access.This reduces the possibility of delay due to weather issues since they will have decreased distances of travel for products and components. Many businesses are making this shift, and in 2021 it was reported Stanley Black & Decker was accelerating plans for [two new factories in Mexico and one in Texas](https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2021/11/15/supply-chains-companies-strategy/). After ports closed and freight costs grew seven-fold, having closer points of supply became important to the toolmaker’s business.Similarly, in 2022, Toyota announced plans to expand its manufacturing operations in the United States, focusing on building new facilities in Alabama and Kentucky. The company made this decision after facing significant supply chain disruptions due to global shipping delays and increasing freight costs. By moving production closer to its core market, Toyota aimed to reduce transportation times and costs while ensuring a more resilient supply chain to meet growing customer demand.  Regional suppliers may be more expensive, but reduced travel time and lowered exposure to risk offset those costs.

No one says you have to only source from onshore or domestic providers. Keeping a good mix of providers and performing appropriate due diligence each year protects an organization from one-supplier dependencies.

### 3. Maintain Inventory Buffers

The just-in-time (JIT) supply chain creates cost savings by reducing warehousing costs. However, some industry analysts hypothesize businesses have cut too deep into this space and now need to build back stock. The added expense of [adopting a “just in case” approach](https://www.csrwire.com/press_releases/724466-just-time-just-case-rethinking-supply-chain-strategy) may be worthwhile, as companies are better positioned to maintain product flow and business continuity during unexpected weather events or other rare occurrences thanks to the backup stockpiles.

Again, establishing a baseline of supply chain metrics from prior years can help here, providing stakeholders with historical data that can be used to make predictions and estimates about future inventory needs.

### 4. Improve Vendor Visibility

Understanding all parts of your supply chain can help identify potential problems before they happen. [It’s important to make sure you have good visibility into all of your third-party vendors](https://www.auditboard.com/blog/murky-visibility-across-the-supply-chain-how-organizations-are-overcoming-tprm-roadblocks/), including their financial standing, and their potential outside dependencies. Review major credit rating agency reports on potential suppliers. Look for technology that provides product and shipment visibility to keep yourself and your customers current on expected delivery times. Lastly, you should always make sure to undertake a thorough supply chain risk assessment before signing any contracts.

Consider developing a due diligence and procurement lifecycle for your SCRM programs. This involves setting up policies and procedures for how to perform initial due diligence, requirements for procurement, and a set frequency, like annually, for evaluating vendors and third parties based on their criticality to business operations. Periodic audits on the suppliers can provide further transparency and insight into a provider’s compliance, operations, security, and governance.

### 5. Model Worst-Case Scenarios

A supply chain risk management framework must take into account what a company’s response will be when the worst happens. Thanks to big data, predictive analytics, and data modeling, companies should have enough information to simulate high-risk events and their impact. Using modeling to forecast nightmare scenarios allows businesses to develop contingency plans, fallbacks, and communication workflows for how to proceed in the event a disaster strikes.

Performing tabletop exercises (TTX) for likely events or scenarios where personnel simulate an incident and response in real-time prepares the organization for future response activities. These are especially effective for scenarios that require extensive coordination and immediate action.

### 6. Find Software Solutions

SCRM software enables companies to get ahead in risk management by improving visibility into a company’s entire supply chain ecosystem. With a better understanding of your supply chain, you’ll be able to quickly determine weak areas and also receive data-driven insights on potential improvements. Using streamlined software and technology for different areas of the business also creates improved flexibility in the event of supply chain disruptions.

Implementing [cloud-based software](https://www.auditboard.com/blog/what-are-the-security-risks-of-cloud-computing/) throughout your company’s entire network reduces inefficiencies and better positions your business for potential outages due to redundancies and shared data, though diversification of providers is also important. Another benefit of SCRM software is it provides complete visibility into a company’s supply chain, allowing business owners and managers to easily spot unusual activity. The upside is obvious in the case of a catastrophic event, but it’s also helpful in the day-to-day when business leads are looking for areas of opportunity for improved efficiencies, cost savings, and increased profitability.

### 7. Perform Regular Supply Chain Risk Assessments

Regularly performed risk assessments serve as an important anchor in an SCRM program.

By conducting a risk assessment of your SCRM strategy, your organization can streamline risk identification, analysis, mitigation, and monitoring, while also updating your risk register and creating effective action plans and mitigation strategies.Risk assessments can be performed by [internal audit](https://www.auditboard.com/blog/internal-audit-101/) teams or by external auditors. Either approach is valid and productive, though organizations may want to consider seeking out third-party auditors every two to three years in order to garner opinions and recommendations from an independent party.

Organizations should also establish a cadence for reviewing existing suppliers and providers, at least annually. During this review, the business can adjust a vendor’s criticality rating, decide whether to continue retaining that supplier and/or increase or decrease procurement volumes from that vendor.

With these seven steps, your organization can optimize its SCRM program and build a more efficient, effective supply chain.

## Ready to Elevate Your Supply Chain Risk Management Program?

Supply chain risk management is a hefty discipline and effort on its own. Managing due diligence, supplier assessments, vendor criticality ratings, SCRM risk assessments, vendor relationships, and periodic supplier reviews is daunting and difficult. Collaborating with company stakeholders and ensuring that assigned risk mitigation activities are taking place is the icing on the cake. All of this can be taken on with the help of a [risk management software solution](https://www.auditboard.com/product/risk-management/), centralizing your SCRM program, stakeholder communications, and key information. Don’t go it alone — leverage technology to optimize your supply chain [risk management strategy](/blog/10-risk-management-strategies).

## Frequently Asked Questions About Supply Chain Risk Management

### What is supply chain risk management?

Supply chain risk management is the process by which an enterprise takes strategic steps to identify, assess, and mitigate all risks in its business’s end-to-end supply chain, including the procurement of raw materials and the cyber supply chain.

### Why is supply chain risk management important?

Supply chain risk management is important because it gives companies greater insight into their supply chains, enables the prevention of supply chain disruption, and provides mitigation and recovery strategies in the event of a supply chain disruption.

### What are best practices for managing supply chain risk?

Using multiple suppliers, finding closer sources, knowing your risk tolerance, and modeling dire scenarios are all tactics and methodologies for building a solid risk management plan.
